range: Covenant faithfulness that goes beyond legal obligation. Not mere feeling but committed action — the word that holds the Bible together.
note: Used 248 times in the OT. Describes God’s character more than any other single word. Often paired with ‘emet’ (faithfulness) to express covenant reliability.

occurrences.ctx: Abraham’s servant prays for God’s hesed
The LORD showed Joseph hesed in prison
Song of the Sea: ‘In your hesed you will lead the people you redeemed’
God’s self-revelation: ‘abounding in hesed and faithfulness’
Naomi: ‘May the LORD show hesed to you’
Boaz: ‘This hesed is greater than the first’
Davidic covenant: ‘My hesed will not depart from him’
Solomon’s prayer: ‘You who keep your covenant of hesed’
The LORD was gracious because of his hesed with Abraham, Isaac and Jacob

range: Far more than absence of conflict. Shalom is the state of things being as they should be — creation functioning as God intended. It is the goal of the entire biblical story.
note: From the root sh-l-m (to be complete, whole). Related to the verb shillem (to repay, make restitution) and the name Shlomo (Solomon, ‘his peace’).

occurrences.ctx: Abraham: ‘You will go to your ancestors in shalom’
Jacob sends Joseph to check on the shalom of his brothers
Aaronic blessing: ‘The LORD give you shalom’
Gideon builds altar: Yahweh-Shalom
Solomon had shalom on all sides
Angels: ‘peace on earth’ — shalom fulfilled

range: Not ‘law’ in the modern legal sense but ‘instruction’ — a parent teaching a child how to walk. Torah is God’s loving guidance for life in covenant relationship.
note: From the root y-r-h (to throw, to shoot, to teach). The same root gives ‘moreh’ (teacher). Torah is relational before it is legal.

occurrences.ctx: God gives Moses the torah written on stone
‘What nation has such righteous torah?’
Teach them to your children — torah as household practice
‘Do not let this Book of the Torah depart from your mouth’
‘Do not forsake your mother’s torah’
‘This is the torah of the burnt offering, grain offering, sin offering’
"I have found the Book of the Torah in the temple" — Hilkiah's discovery

range: A solemn binding agreement between parties. In the OT, God initiates covenants with humanity — not negotiated contracts but gracious commitments by the greater party.
note: Appears 287 times. The concept structures the entire Bible: Noahic, Abrahamic, Mosaic, Davidic, and New covenants.

occurrences.ctx: God establishes his berith with Noah
The LORD made a berith with Abram
‘An everlasting berith between me and you’
‘If you keep my berith, you will be my treasured possession’
Moses reads the Book of the berith
The Davidic berith — eternal throne
Solomon: ‘You keep your berith of love’
‘I will keep my berith with you’
Josiah renews the berith before the LORD — the last great covenant renewal
Josiah renews the covenant before the LORD — the last great reform
David’s psalm: ‘He remembers his berith forever, the promise he made for a thousand generations’

range: In ANE context, kings placed their tselem (statue/image) in conquered territories to represent their rule. Humanity as God’s tselem means every person is God’s representative on earth.
note: Used in Gen 1:26-27 for ‘image of God’ (tselem elohim). This is the theological foundation of human dignity, equality, and the prohibition against murder (Gen 9:6).

occurrences.ctx: ‘Let us make mankind in our tselem’
‘In the tselem of God he created them; male and female’
Adam fathered Seth in his own tselem
‘For in the tselem of God has God made mankind’

range: Simultaneously wind, breath, and spirit. The ambiguity is theologically productive: God’s ruach is the invisible animating force behind all life — cosmic, personal, and divine.
note: In Gen 1:2 the ruach of God hovers over the waters. In Judg 14:6 ruach rushes on Samson. In 1 Kgs 19:11 God is not in the great wind (ruach). The same word carries all three meanings.

occurrences.ctx: The ruach of God was hovering over the waters
God breathed into his nostrils the ruach of life
‘My ruach will not contend with humans forever’
The LORD took of the ruach on Moses and put it on the elders
The ruach of the LORD came on Othniel
The ruach of the LORD rushed on Samson
The ruach of the LORD came on David
A great and powerful ruach tore the mountains apart
Atonement for the tabernacle — where God’s ruach dwells among impurity
The ruach of Elijah rests on Elisha — prophetic succession
"Let me inherit a double portion of your ruach" — Elisha to Elijah
David gave Solomon the plans ‘that the ruach had put in his mind’ — temple blueprints by divine inspiration

range: To rescue from danger, oppression, or death. The root gives us ‘Yeshua’ (Joshua/Jesus) and ‘yeshu’ah’ (salvation). Every rescue in the OT points toward the ultimate deliverance.
note: Joshua (Yehoshua = ‘YHWH saves’) and Jesus (Yeshua) share this root. The angel tells Joseph: ‘He will yasha his people from their sins’ (Matt 1:21).

occurrences.ctx: The LORD yasha’d Israel from the Egyptians
The LORD raised up judges who yasha’d them
David: ‘The battle is the LORD’s and he will yasha’
‘He will save (yōshīaʻ) his people from their sins’
I will defend this city and save (yasha) it, for my sake and for David’s sake

range: From ordinary speech to cosmic creative force. John 1 uses it in a way that resonated with both Jewish (dabar/memra) and Greek (Stoic logos) audiences simultaneously.
note: In John’s prologue, Logos is a title for Christ — the self-expression of God. This is the most philosophically dense opening in the NT.

occurrences.ctx: ‘In the beginning was the Logos’
‘The Logos became flesh and dwelt among us’
‘Those who accepted his logos were baptized’
‘The logos of God spread’

range: Self-giving, unconditional love. In the NT, agape is not primarily an emotion but a decision to act for another’s good regardless of cost. It describes God’s love for the world and the love Christians are called to show.
note: Distinguished from philía (friendship love) and erōs (romantic love). Agape is chosen, costly, and directed at the undeserving. It defines God’s character (1 John 4:8).

occurrences.ctx: ‘God so agape’d the world’
‘A new commandment: agapate one another’
‘Greater agape has no one than this: to lay down one’s life’
The early church devoted to agape fellowship

range: Not intellectual assent but wholehearted trust and loyalty. Pistis is relational: it means entrusting yourself to another person. In the NT, it is the proper human response to God’s action in Christ.
note: Pistis can mean both the human act of trusting and God’s own faithfulness. This double meaning runs through Romans and Galatians.

occurrences.ctx: ‘I have not found such pistis in Israel’
‘If you have pistis as small as a mustard seed’
‘Have pistis in God’
‘Your pistis has saved you’
Stephen, ‘full of pistis and the Holy Spirit’

range: Not primarily a place but a reign — God’s active rule breaking into human history. ‘The kingdom of God has come near’ means God is acting now, not that a territory has been established.
note: Central to Jesus’ preaching. Matthew uses ‘kingdom of heaven’ (Jewish reverence avoids saying ‘God’); Mark and Luke use ‘kingdom of God.’ Same concept.

occurrences.ctx: ‘The basileia of heaven has come near’
‘Seek first his basileia and his righteousness’
‘The basileia of heaven is like a mustard seed’
‘The basileia of God has come near’
‘The basileia of God is in your midst’
Jesus spoke about the basileia of God for forty days
Paul proclaimed the basileia of God boldly in Rome

range: The Greek equivalent of Hebrew ruach. Same productive ambiguity: wind, breath, and divine Spirit all in one word. In the NT, pneuma most often refers to the Holy Spirit — the presence and power of God active in believers.
note: Jesus tells Nicodemus: ‘The pneuma blows where it wills’ (John 3:8) — deliberately exploiting the wind/Spirit double meaning. At Pentecost, the Spirit arrives as rushing wind (Acts 2:2).

occurrences.ctx: The pneuma of God descending like a dove at Jesus’ baptism
‘The pneuma blows where it wishes’
Jesus breathed on them: ‘Receive the Holy pneuma’
Pentecost: rushing pneuma fills them, they speak in tongues

range: Right relationship with God and right conduct toward others. Includes both the legal standing of being declared righteous and the lived practice of doing justice. These two senses are never separated in biblical thought.
note: Matthew uses it 7 times — more than any other Gospel. It is the ethic of the kingdom: ‘unless your dikaiosyne surpasses that of the Pharisees’ (Matt 5:20).

occurrences.ctx: ‘Blessed are those who hunger and thirst for dikaiosyne’
‘Unless your dikaiosyne surpasses the Pharisees’
‘Seek first his kingdom and his dikaiosyne’
Serving God in holiness and dikaiosyne
‘In every nation the one who does dikaiosyne is acceptable’

range: The Chronicler’s signature theological verb. To seek God (darash) means to orient one’s life, worship, and governance toward YHWH. The opposite is to forsake (ʿazab). Every king in Chronicles is evaluated by whether they sought or forsook God.
note: The verb appears over 40 times in Chronicles. It carries the sense of intentional pursuit, not passive waiting. Seeking God is the active choice that determines destiny in the Chronicler’s theology.

occurrences.ctx: David’s psalm: ‘Seek the LORD and his strength; seek his face always.’
‘If my people, who are called by my name, will humble themselves and pray and seek my face...’
Azariah to Asa: ‘If you seek him, he will be found by you; if you forsake him, he will forsake you.’
Uzziah ‘sought God during the days of Zechariah... as long as he sought the LORD, God gave him success.’

range: To be firm, reliable, trustworthy. In the hiphil: to believe, put trust in. The root of 'amen' and 'emunah' (faithfulness).
note: Used 108 times. Abraham 'believed' (heʾĕmîn) God in Gen 15:6 — the foundational OT text on faith, quoted by Paul in Rom 4:3 and Gal 3:6.

occurrences.ctx: Abram believed the LORD, and he credited it to him as righteousness.
Israel saw the great power and put their trust in the LORD and in Moses.
If you do not stand firm in your faith, you will not stand at all.
The righteous person will live by his faithfulness — quoted in Rom 1:17.

range: Fundamentally means 'separate, distinct, other.' Applied to God it denotes his utter otherness; applied to Israel it means set apart for God's purposes.
note: Used 116 times. Isaiah's trisagion ('Holy, holy, holy' — Isa 6:3) is the only divine attribute repeated three times in Scripture, emphasizing God's supreme otherness.

occurrences.ctx: Be holy because I, the LORD your God, am holy.
Holy, holy, holy is the LORD Almighty; the whole earth is full of his glory.
Exalt the LORD our God and worship at his holy mountain, for the LORD our God is holy.
I am the LORD, your Holy One, Israel's Creator, your King.

range: From the root š-p-ṭ (to judge). Encompasses legal rulings, social justice, proper order, and the rights owed to every person — especially the vulnerable.
note: Used 425 times — one of the most frequent words in the OT. Often paired with tsedaqah (righteousness). Micah 6:8 makes it one of God's three core requirements.

occurrences.ctx: What does the LORD require of you? To act justly and to love mercy and to walk humbly.
Learn to do right; seek justice. Defend the oppressed.
Righteousness and justice are the foundation of your throne.
Follow justice and justice alone, so that you may live and possess the land.

range: From the noun reḥem (womb). God's compassion is visceral, maternal — a love rooted in the deepest physical bond humans know.
note: Used 47 times in verbal form. The related noun raḥămîm ('compassion') appears 40 times. The womb-connection makes this the most embodied word for divine love in Hebrew.

occurrences.ctx: I will have mercy on whom I will have mercy, and I will have compassion on whom I will have compassion.
Can a mother forget the baby at her breast? Though she may forget, I will not forget you.
As a father has compassion on his children, so the LORD has compassion on those who fear him.
I will show my love to the one I called 'Not my loved one.' Quoted in Rom 9:25.

range: Covers the full spectrum from terror before an enemy to reverent awe before God. 'The fear of the LORD' is not cowering but the orientation of a life that takes God seriously.
note: Used 314 times. 'The fear of the LORD is the beginning of wisdom' (Prov 9:10) — the foundational axiom of Israelite wisdom literature.

occurrences.ctx: The fear of the LORD is the beginning of wisdom, and knowledge of the Holy One is understanding.
Now I know that you fear God, because you have not withheld your son.
Fear the LORD, you his holy people, for those who fear him lack nothing.
The Spirit of the LORD will rest on him — the Spirit of knowledge and fear of the LORD.

range: To act as a gōʾēl — the nearest relative who restores what was lost. This could mean buying back property, freeing a slave, avenging blood, or marrying a widow. God himself is Israel's Gōʾēl.
note: Used 104 times. The book of Ruth is built on this concept. Isaiah uses gōʾēl for God 13 times. Job's 'I know that my Redeemer lives' (19:25) bridges personal and cosmic redemption.

occurrences.ctx: Boaz acts as kinsman-redeemer for Naomi's land and Ruth's future.
This is what the LORD says — Israel's King and Redeemer, the LORD Almighty.
I know that my Redeemer lives, and that in the end he will stand on the earth.
You, LORD, are our Father, our Redeemer from of old is your name.

range: Likely from 'to cover' or 'to wipe clean.' The Day of Atonement (Yom Kippur) derives from this root. The kappōret (mercy seat/atonement cover) sat on the ark.
note: Used 102 times. Central to Levitical theology. The LXX translates the kappōret as hilastērion — the same word Paul uses for Christ in Rom 3:25.

occurrences.ctx: On this day atonement will be made for you, to cleanse you from all your sins before the LORD.
It is the blood that makes atonement for one's life.
Make an atonement cover (kappōret) of pure gold.
To atone for wickedness, to bring in everlasting righteousness.

range: One who speaks on behalf of another — specifically, on behalf of God. The nābîʾ receives God's word and delivers it, whether as prediction, exhortation, judgment, or comfort.
note: Used 317 times. Moses is the prototypical prophet (Deut 18:15-18). The prophetic office spans from Abraham (Gen 20:7) to Malachi. Jesus is the 'prophet like Moses' (Acts 3:22).

occurrences.ctx: I will raise up for them a prophet like you from among their fellow Israelites.
Surely the Sovereign LORD does nothing without revealing his plan to his servants the prophets.
Formerly in Israel, if someone wanted to inquire of God, they would say, 'Let us go to the seer.'
Before you were born I set you apart; I appointed you as a prophet to the nations.

range: The same word means to serve a master, work the ground, and worship God. In Hebrew thought, service and worship are not separate categories — to serve God is to worship; to worship is to serve.
note: Used 290 times. Adam was placed in Eden 'to work (ʿābad) and keep it' (Gen 2:15) — the same verbs later used for priestly service in the tabernacle (Num 3:7-8).

occurrences.ctx: The LORD God took the man and put him in the Garden of Eden to work it and take care of it.
When you have brought the people out of Egypt, you will worship God on this mountain.
Choose for yourselves this day whom you will serve — as for me and my household, we will serve the LORD.
Worship the LORD with gladness; come before him with joyful songs.

range: One who conforms to the standard of right relationship — with God and with neighbor. Not mere moral perfection but relational fidelity and covenantal integrity.
note: Used 206 times. Abraham's debate with God over Sodom (Gen 18:23-25) turns entirely on this word. 'The righteous shall live by his faith' (Hab 2:4) became Paul's anthem in Romans.

occurrences.ctx: Will you sweep away the righteous with the wicked?
The righteous person will live by his faithfulness.
The LORD watches over the way of the righteous, but the way of the wicked leads to destruction.
When the storm has swept by, the wicked are gone, but the righteous stand firm forever.

range: In Hebrew, to truly hear is to obey. The Shema (Deut 6:4) is both a call to listen and a command to respond. There is no hearing without doing.
note: Used 1,159 times — one of the most frequent verbs in the OT. The Shema ('Hear, O Israel') is the foundational confession of Jewish faith, recited daily.

occurrences.ctx: Hear, O Israel: The LORD our God, the LORD is one.
To obey is better than sacrifice, and to heed is better than the fat of rams.
Be ever hearing, but never understanding — quoted by Jesus in Matt 13:14.
But my people would not listen to me; Israel would not submit to me.

range: The basic meaning is to miss a target or deviate from the path. Sin is not merely rule-breaking but a failure to hit the goal God set for human life.
note: Used 238 times. First appears in Gen 4:7 where sin 'crouches at the door' — personified as a predator. The 'missing' metaphor shapes all subsequent biblical hamartiology.

occurrences.ctx: If you do not do what is right, sin is crouching at your door; it desires to have you.
Against you, you only, have I sinned and done what is evil in your sight.
You have committed a great sin. But now I will go up to the LORD; perhaps I can make atonement.
Among all these soldiers there were 700 who could sling a stone and not miss — literal 'missing' usage.

range: Not abstract infinity but time stretching beyond the horizon in either direction — the remote past or the indefinite future. God's covenant is 'everlasting' (bĕrît ʿôlām).
note: Used 439 times. Eccl 3:11 says God 'set eternity in the human heart' — using ʿôlām to describe the restless longing for transcendence that defines humanity.

occurrences.ctx: I will establish my covenant as an everlasting covenant between me and you.
He has also set eternity in the human heart; yet no one can fathom what God has done.
From everlasting to everlasting you are God.
The LORD is the everlasting God, the Creator of the ends of the earth.

range: Unmerited favor — a gift given freely, not earned. In classical Greek it meant charm or beauty; the NT transforms it into the defining word for God's saving action through Christ.
note: Used 155 times in the NT. Paul uses it 100 times. Eph 2:8-9 is the locus classicus: 'by grace you have been saved, through faith — and this is not from yourselves, it is the gift of God.'

occurrences.ctx: The Word became flesh — full of grace and truth.
It is by grace you have been saved, through faith — not from yourselves, it is the gift of God.
All are justified freely by his grace through the redemption in Christ Jesus.
My grace is sufficient for you, for my power is made perfect in weakness.

range: From kyros (authority, power). Ranges from polite address ('sir') to supreme divine title. The LXX used kyrios to translate YHWH — so when the NT calls Jesus kyrios, it is making a divine claim.
note: Used 717 times in the NT. The confession 'Jesus is Lord' (kyrios Iēsous, Rom 10:9) is the earliest Christian creed and a direct claim of deity via the YHWH-kyrios connection.

occurrences.ctx: If you declare with your mouth, 'Jesus is Lord,' and believe in your heart — you will be saved.
Every tongue acknowledge that Jesus Christ is Lord, to the glory of God the Father.
God has made this Jesus, whom you crucified, both Lord and Messiah.
No one can say 'Jesus is Lord' except by the Holy Spirit.

range: From sōzō (to save/deliver). Encompasses physical rescue, spiritual redemption, and eschatological hope. In the NT it covers past justification, present sanctification, and future glorification.
note: Used 45 times. The Hebrew equivalent is yĕšûʿāh — the root of the name Jesus (Yēšûaʿ). 'You are to give him the name Jesus, because he will save his people from their sins' (Matt 1:21).

occurrences.ctx: Salvation is found in no one else, for there is no other name under heaven given to mankind by which we must be saved.
It is by grace you have been saved.
Continue to work out your salvation with fear and trembling.
The gospel is the power of God that brings salvation to everyone who believes.

range: From meta (after/change) + nous (mind). More than regret — a fundamental reorientation of the whole person. The Hebrew equivalent is šûb (to turn/return).
note: Used 22 times. Both John the Baptist and Jesus open their ministries with the same word: 'Repent (metanoeite), for the kingdom of heaven has come near' (Matt 3:2, 4:17).

occurrences.ctx: Repent, for the kingdom of heaven has come near.
Repent and be baptized, every one of you, in the name of Jesus Christ for the forgiveness of your sins.
There will be more rejoicing in heaven over one sinner who repents than over ninety-nine righteous persons.
Godly sorrow brings repentance that leads to salvation and leaves no regret.

range: From ek (out) + kaleō (to call). Not a building but a called-out people. In secular Greek it meant a civic assembly of citizens. The LXX used it to translate qāhāl (assembly of Israel).
note: Used 114 times. Jesus uses it only twice (Matt 16:18, 18:17). Paul uses it 62 times. The word carries both the local sense (house church) and the universal sense (all believers).

occurrences.ctx: On this rock I will build my church, and the gates of Hades will not overcome it.
The Lord added to their number daily those who were being saved.
Christ loved the church and gave himself up for her.
Now you are the body of Christ, and each one of you is a part of it.

range: From eu (good) + angelos (messenger). In Roman usage it announced imperial victories or a new emperor's accession. The early Christians deliberately used this political word for the news of Christ's kingdom.
note: Used 76 times. Mark opens his Gospel: 'The beginning of the euangelion of Jesus Christ, the Son of God' — a direct challenge to Rome's euangelia about Caesar.

occurrences.ctx: The beginning of the good news about Jesus the Messiah, the Son of God.
I am not ashamed of the gospel, because it is the power of God for salvation.
The gospel I preached to you: that Christ died for our sins, was buried, and was raised on the third day.
I am astonished that you are so quickly deserting the one who called you — there is no other gospel.

range: In John's Gospel, zōē is not mere biological existence (bios) but the divine quality of life that comes from knowing God. 'Eternal life' (zōē aiōnios) is not primarily about duration but about participation in God's own life.
note: Used 135 times. John uses it 36 times — more than any other NT author. Jesus says 'I am the way, the truth, and the life (zōē)' — identifying himself as the source of this divine quality.

occurrences.ctx: I am the way and the truth and the life. No one comes to the Father except through me.
I have come that they may have life, and have it to the full.
This is eternal life: that they know you, the only true God, and Jesus Christ.
Whoever has the Son has life; whoever does not have the Son of God does not have life.

range: Covers the full spectrum from terror to reverent awe. 'The fear of the LORD' (yirʾat YHWH) is the beginning of wisdom — not cringing dread but the proper posture before the holy.
note: Used over 300 times in the OT. The phrase 'fear not' (ʾal-tîrāʾ) appears 70+ times as a divine reassurance. The same root that commands reverence also comforts against terror — context determines everything.

occurrences.ctx: Now I know that you fear God
The fear of the LORD is the beginning of knowledge
The fear of the LORD is the beginning of wisdom
Do not fear, for I am with you
